💳 cards  
   give any agent its own debit card: fund a wallet, set a budget, and issue one-time cards that auto-close after a single charge. if something goes wrong, the blast radius is one capped card, not your bank account.  
   

 🛒 buy (new — launching today!)  
   you don't even wire up a merchant. connect once, then tell your agent "buy me sushi" and it runs the whole thing — search, cart, checkout, payment — in the conversation. first up: DoorDash, right from inside Claude. it feels like magic 🥹  
   

 🏢 Agentcard for companies  
   drop Agentcard into your own product and give your users' agents their own cards at scale, with budgets and spend controls built in. [...] See original launch post

hey everyone, we're Karen and Felipe, and we are building Agentcard: debit cards for AI agents.

# TL;DR

AI agents are finally smart enough to do real things for you. but the second they need to pay for something, they're stuck, and handing an agent your real card (no limits, no controls) is a terrible idea.

so we built Agentcard: give your agent its own debit card: fund a wallet, set a budget, and issue one-time cards that auto-close after a single charge. works with any agent over MCP, the CLI, or our API. its everything an agent needs to pay for things online. here's the lineup:

so we built Agentcard — everything an agent needs to pay for things online. here's the lineup

# the team [...] # the team

Karen (CEO): former CEO @ Atlas (acquired by Remote), then turned down a job at Stripe to build Agentcard instead.

Felipe (CTO): second-time YC founder (HelloGuru, W22) and first employee at Rappi (YC W16), where he shipped consumer products at scale.

# the problem

agents can now research, plan, and decide but the second they need to pay, they're stuck. the workarounds are all bad: hand your agent your real card and you've got no limits and no controls (one bad prompt from disaster), or build your own issuing + checkout stack and lose months becoming a payments company. so agents do the first 90% and hand checkout back to you, the most valuable step is the one they can't take.

# the solution

Agentcard is everything an agent needs to pay for things online:

## A payment method your agents can use today

Give agents a way to pay for things  
online on behalf of users.

### One-time cards

Single-use cards that self-destruct after one payment. Card credentials never touch your servers.

$ curl -X POST 

-H "Authorization: Bearer $AGENTCARD\_API\_KEY" \

-H "Content-Type: application/json" \

-d '{"amountCents": 5000}'

### Human authorization

Set budgets, per-transaction limits, and approval thresholds for your users. You stay in control of what agents can spend.

Hey human:)

results.title: Why Your AI Agent Needs Its Own Wallet (And Why a ...
results.content:
AgentCard: One Task, One Card

The second product I use constantly is FluxA AgentCard.

The concept is beautifully simple: every time your agent needs to make a payment at a service that only accepts traditional card payments (Stripe checkout, Shopify, etc.), it generates a single-use virtual card funded with a specific amount.

$ fluxa-wallet card create --amount 25.00 --mandate mand\_abc123

The card gets created on the spot, the agent uses it, and when the transaction settles — the card is permanently invalidated. No lingering credentials. No reuse risk. The unused balance returns to your wallet automatically.

Compare this to giving an agent your real card:

Risk

Real Card

FluxA AgentCard

Spending scope

Full credit line exposed

Amount-locked per card

Reuse risk [...] Step 2: Fund it with USDC

FluxA operates in USDC. Deposit what you need. I keep a working balance of ~$50-100 for my active agents.

Step 3: Create a mandate for your agent

A mandate is a spending budget with a defined purpose. Example:

{  
"amount": 100.00,  
"intent": "Automate marketing ops spend for Q2 campaign",  
"duration": "30d"  
}

Step 4: Connect your agent

FluxA exposes an MCP server and SDK. If you're using Claude, LangChain, CrewAI, or AutoGen, the integration is straightforward:

npx skills add -s agentic-checkout -y -g FluxA-Agent-Payment/FluxA-AI-Wallet-MCP

Your agent now has access to:

wallet.balance — check available funds

card.create — issue a single-use AgentCard

payment.request — request a mandate from the human operator [...] How Intent-Pay Works

Instead of approving every single payment, you approve a spending intent once:

You → Agent: "Research and buy 3 domain names for my new project. Budget: $50."  
Agent → FluxA: "I need a mandate for up to $50 for domain purchases."  
You → FluxA: [approve once]  
Agent → Executes 3 payments autonomously within the $50 mandate

That's it. One signature, multiple payments. The agent keeps moving. You don't get interrupted.

And here's the critical part: FluxA's risk engine runs on every transaction. If the agent tries to spend outside the approved intent — say, buying something unrelated — it gets blocked at the wallet level. Not by the agent. Not by a prompt. By the infrastructure.

AgentCard: One Task, One Card
